perturbation.original_text: In the event that the Receiving Party should breach any of the provisions of this Agreement and f ail to remedy such breach within 7 (seven) business days from date of a written notice to do so, then the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to invoke all remedies available to it in law including, but not limited to, the institution of urgent proceedings as well as any other way of relief appropriate under the circumstances, in any court of competent jurisdiction, in the event of breach or threatened breach of the Agreement and/or an action for damages.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that the Receiving Party should breach any of the provisions of this Agreement and f ail to remedy such breach within 2 (two) business days from date of a written notice to do so, then the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to invoke all remedies available to it in law including, but not limited to, the institution of urgent proceedings as well as any other way of relief appropriate under the circumstances, in any court of competent jurisdiction, in the event of breach or threatened breach of the Agreement and/or an action for damages.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the cure period for a breach of contract from 7 days to 2 days could be considered unreasonably short under South African law, potentially conflicting with principles of fairness and equity in contract enforcement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The Conventional Penalties Act 15 of 1962, Section 3
perturbation.law_explanation: The Conventional Penalties Act allows courts to reduce penalty stipulations if they appear unreasonably high, meaning that a court could deem a 2-day remedy period as an unfair penalty for breach, especially when compared to the standard 7 days, and therefore unenforceable.
perturbation.justification: The perturbation explanation and the law explanation both suggest the reduction of the cure period from 7 to 2 days could be considered unreasonably short and an unfair penalty under South African law. This aligns with the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition, where a time-sensitive obligation (cure period) is altered in a way that potentially violates legal principles of fairness, making the contractual term unenforceable. While no specific law snippets are provided, the explanations point to the Conventional Penalties Act, which allows courts to reduce penalty stipulations if they appear unreasonably high. Thus, reducing the remedy period could be seen as creating an unfair penalty for breach.

perturbation.original_text: Any notice given by one party to the other is deemed to have been received by the addressee: 20.2.1. on the date on which the same was delivered to the addressee's address if delivered by ha nd; or 20.2.2. on the 7th (seventh) calendar day after the date of posting if sent by pre - paid registered post to the addressee's address; or
perturbation.changed_text: Any notice given by one party to the other is deemed to have been received by the addressee: 20.2.1. on the date on which the same was delivered to the addressee's address if delivered by ha nd; or 20.2.2. on the 30th (thirtieth) calendar day after the date of posting if sent by pre - paid registered post to the addressee's address; or
perturbation.explanation: Specifying a 30-day deemed receipt period for postal notices is unreasonably long and could contradict implied terms of reasonableness and good faith, given postal services are much faster in South Africa, potentially conflicting with legal principles of contract interpretation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Consumer Protection Act, 2008 (Act No. 68 of 2008), Section 48
perturbation.law_citation: Consumer Protection Act, 2008 (Act No. 68 of 2008), Section 48
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 48 prohibits unfair, unreasonable or unjust contract terms. A 30-day deemed notice period by post is arguably unfair to the sender and would be seen as unjust since postal service in South Africa takes significantly less time than 30 days.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies a 7-day period for deemed receipt of notices sent by registered post, whereas the changed text extends this period to 30 days. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both argue that a 30-day period is unreasonable and unfair in the South African context, given the postal service's efficiency. This change introduces an inconsistency in the contract that could be interpreted as violating the implied terms of reasonableness and good faith, thus contradicting legal principles of contract interpretation. Although no specific law snippet directly states the illegality, the explanations suggest that the change would be seen as unjust under South African law.

perturbation.original_text: Eskom at its sole discretion may elect whether the Counterparty can continue with the Project fully or partially and to the extent the Counterparty cannot continue with the Project it will renounce any benefits it may have obtained had it completed the Project and will be paid only for work completed.
perturbation.changed_text: Eskom at its sole discretion may elect whether the Counterparty can continue with the Project fully or partially and to the extent the Counterparty cannot continue with the Project it will renounce any benefits it may have obtained had it completed the Project and will be paid nothing, even for work completed.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the clause to state that the Counterparty will be paid nothing, even for work completed, contradicts the principles of unjust enrichment and fair compensation under South African contract law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: South African Law of Unjustified Enrichment
perturbation.law_citation: South African Law of Unjustified Enrichment
perturbation.law_explanation: While there's no single statute, South African common law prohibits unjust enrichment. If Eskom receives a benefit from the work completed by the Counterparty, refusing to pay anything constitutes unjust enrichment, and the Counterparty would have a claim for compensation based on the value of the work done, even if the contract is terminated early.

perturbation.justification: The modified text explicitly states that the Counterparty will be paid 'nothing, even for work completed.' This directly contradicts the principle against unjust enrichment, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is broad and doesn't explicitly address unjust enrichment, the Law Explanation provides the legal basis for this principle under South African common law. The Perturbation Explanation reinforces this contradiction by stating that refusing payment for completed work constitutes unjust enrichment, which is legally problematic.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that the Receiving Party should breach any of the provisions of this Agreement and f ail to remedy such breach within 7 (seven) business days from date of a written notice to do so, then the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to invoke all remedies available to it in law including, but not limited to, the institution of urgent proceedings as well as any other way of relief appropriate under the circumstances, in any court of competent jurisdiction, in the event of breach or threatened breach of the Agreement and/or an action for damages.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that the Receiving Party should breach any of the provisions of this Agreement, the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to invoke all remedies available to it in law immediately, without any notice or cure period, including, but not limited to, the institution of urgent proceedings as well as any other way of relief appropriate under the circumstances, in any court of competent jurisdiction, in the event of breach or threatened breach of the Agreement and/or an action for damages.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the cure period entirely contradicts principles of fairness and good faith in South African contract law, potentially conflicting with legal requirements for reasonable notice and an opportunity to remedy a breach before legal action.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Principles of Natural Justice in South African Law
perturbation.law_explanation: While not statutory, principles of natural justice (audi alteram partem) require fairness in legal proceedings. Removing the right to be heard or given an opportunity to remedy a breach goes against these principles. South African courts often imply a requirement for reasonable notice, and the complete removal of a remedy period would likely be considered unfair.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Natural justice | law | Britannica Ask the Chatbot Games & Quizzes History & Society Science & Tech Biographies Animals & Nature Geography & Travel Arts & Culture ProCon Money Videos natural justice Directory References Contents natural justice law Ask the Chatbot a Question Ask the Chatbot a Question Learn about this topic in these articles: influence on administrative procedure In administrative law: Administrative procedure …common-law systems, the doctrine of natural justice influences administrative procedure in two ways: (1) that a person may not be judge of his own cause, and (2) that a person shall not be dealt with to his material disadvantage, whether of person or property, or removed from or disqualified for… Read More
perturbation.justification: The original text provides a 7-day cure period for breaches, whereas the changed text eliminates this cure period entirely. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation argue this removal contradicts principles of fairness and natural justice (audi alteram partem), potentially conflicting with legal requirements for reasonable notice and opportunity to remedy a breach. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines natural justice as ensuring a person shall not be dealt with to his material disadvantage. Removing the cure period potentially puts the Receiving Party at a material disadvantage, supporting the existence of a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be binding on and shall inure for the benefit of the successors and permitted assigns and personal representatives (as the case may be) of the Parties.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be binding only on the Parties signing this document and shall not inure to the benefit of any successors, assigns, or personal representatives, regardless of circumstances.
perturbation.explanation: Restricting the agreement from benefiting successors or assigns directly contradicts established principles of contract law regarding the transferability of contractual rights and obligations under South African law, particularly in the context of business transfers or deceased estates.
perturbation.law_explanation: While section 19 primarily deals with the legal personality of companies, denying the succession of rights in all circumstances (e.g., business rescue, liquidation) is against the common understanding of legal succession and could impede corporate restructuring or liquidation proceedings regulated by the Companies Act and other related legislations.
